coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of alginate/chitosan nanocomposite sponges using green synthesized carbon dots as potential wound dressing
coredata.dc:creator: Rezaei, A.
Ehtesabi, H.
coredata.dc:description: Sponges made with biopolymers have found great potential in biomedical applications due to their good biocompatibility, high flexibility, and extraordinary porosity. But some of their features still need to be improved. In this work, a new nanocomposite sponge with chitosan/alginate/carbon dots (CDs) is introduced. Using electrostatic interaction, calcium chloride as the crosslinking agent, glycerin as a plasticizer, and lyophilization method, alginate and chitosan were bonded with CDs to create these versatile nanocomposite sponges. The results showed that the addition of CDs to chitosan/alginate composite improved the water vapor transmission rate and mechanical properties of nanocomposite sponge. It also increased the porosity, water absorption rate, and hydrophilicity of the nanocomposite sponge which could absorb the moisture in the blood to increase blood concentration and viscosity, and become a semi-swelling viscous colloid to clog the capillaries. Cytocompatibility toxicity tests with L929 cells demonstrated that nanocomposite sponge caused no obvious cytotoxicity. The hemolytic rate of nanocomposite sponges indicates their suitable hemocompatibility. With increasing the concentration of CDs, the in vitro blood-clotting index decreases which indicates an increase in the hemostatic potential of nanocomposite sponges. The better hemostatic effect of nanocomposite sponge may be due to its ability to absorb blood platelets easily and to the higher liquid adsorption ratio. The obtained data encourage the use of this nanocomposite sponge for wound dressings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Natural precursor
Pyracantha
Composite sponge
Wound healing
Hemostasis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the ACCD nanocomposite sponges
Water absorption and water retention capacity
Whole blood clotting tests in vitro
Synthesis and characterization of ACCD nanocomposite sponges
The surface morphology and porous structure
Whole blood clotting tests in vitro

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to those who supported them especially vice presidency for research and technology, Shahid Beheshti University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: One-step route to synthesize CDs by the hydrothermal method with Pyracantha in the presence of DI water.
Schematic of ACCD nanocomposite sponges synthesis method.
(a) Ultraviolet–visible spectrum of CDs, (b) The influence of heat-up time on manufactured CD fluorescence intensity, (c) FTIR spectroscopy of CDs.
(a) Particle size distribution and, (b) TEM image, 40 nm scale, (c) XRD pattern (d) and Raman spectrum of the CDs.
(a) ACCD3 sponges on the right and ACCD0 sponges on the left, (b1-b4) SEM images of the ACCD0 sponge, (c1-c4) SEM images of the ACCD3 nanocomposite sponge.
(a) FT-IR spectra of ACCD0 and ACCD3 sponges, (b) Water contact angles of ACCD sponges.
(a) Porosity, (b) Water absorption, (c) Water retention, (d) and WVTR of the ACCD sponges.
(a) Tensile strength, (b) Elongation at break, (c) Modulus, (d) and bending strain ability of the ACCD sponges.
(a) Cell viability of L929 murine fibroblasts after 24, 48, and 72 h exposure to ACCD0, ACCD1, ACCD2, and ACCD3, (b) The rate of hemolysis for the ACCD samples, (c) The blood clotting test results.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of alginate/chitosan nanocomposite sponges using green synthesized carbon dots as potential wound dressing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sponges made with biopolymers have found great potential in biomedical applications due to their good biocompatibility, high flexibility, and extraordinary porosity. But some of their features still need to be improved. In this work, a new nanocomposite sponge with chitosan/alginate/carbon dots (CDs) is introduced. Using electrostatic interaction, calcium chloride as the crosslinking agent, glycerin as a plasticizer, and lyophilization method, alginate and chitosan were bonded with CDs to create these versatile nanocomposite sponges. The results showed that the addition of CDs to chitosan/alginate composite improved the water vapor transmission rate and mechanical properties of nanocomposite sponge. It also increased the porosity, water absorption rate, and hydrophilicity of the nanocomposite sponge which could absorb the moisture in the blood to increase blood concentration and viscosity, and become a semi-swelling viscous colloid to clog the capillaries. Cytocompatibility toxicity tests with L929 cells demonstrated that nanocomposite sponge caused no obvious cytotoxicity. The hemolytic rate of nanocomposite sponges indicates their suitable hemocompatibility. With increasing the concentration of CDs, the in vitro blood-clotting index decreases which indicates an increase in the hemostatic potential of nanocomposite sponges. The better hemostatic effect of nanocomposite sponge may be due to its ability to absorb blood platelets easily and to the higher liquid adsorption ratio. The obtained data encourage the use of this nanocomposite sponge for wound dressings.

ce:section.ce:para: A wound is any sort of injury to the skin that occurs as a result of a cut or damage. It has a high level of self-regeneration for mild injuries. In serious injuries, however, keeping skin wounds sterile and dry becomes a medical issue. As a result, a wound dressing is required to prevent the loss of fluids and proteins from the wound region, as well as any bacterial invasion that may temporarily replace the function of the skin [1]. Wound dressings should be non-allergenic, non-toxic, and biocompatible to ensure efficient wound healing [2]. High absorption ability, good oxygen permeability, pain relief, ease of removal, anti-infection, wound healing promotion, and reduced scarring are all advantages of an optimal wound dressing material [3]. Up to now, wound dressings have been made from a variety of materials, both synthetic and natural, and in a variety of physical forms such as sponges, hydrogels, foams, hydrocolloids, films, and nanofibers [4]. Sponge dressings with a porous structure have a large porosity and a high specific surface area, which is beneficial for meeting the demands of higher gas permeability, tunable water vapor evaporation, more wound exudate absorption, hemostatic property, and wound infection and dehydration protection [5]. In particular, biopolymer-based sponges are regarded as promising candidates for wound dressings due to their good biocompatibility and biodegradability [6].

Marine polysaccharide-based materials, which are low-cost and plentiful in a variety of biological sources (e.g., marine algae, crustaceans, microbes, and so on), have gotten a lot of attention [7]. The most famous of these biopolymers are chitosan and sodium alginate. D-glucosamine and N-acetylglucosamine are components of chitosan, a natural linear polysaccharide produced by the acetylation of the protein chitin [8]. This compound is known as a hydrophilic, biocompatible, cationic, non-toxic, and biodegradable biopolymer and its antibacterial properties have been confirmed in numerous reports [9,10]. Most importantly, the ability to combine chitosan with other materials and create composites with sponge structures, hydrogels, etc. Is the most interesting feature of this polymer [11]. Sodium alginates are also a group of biopolymers derived from brown seaweed and several bacterial strains [12]. Alginate is a linear anionic polysaccharide consisting of repetitive units of β-D-mannuronic acid (M-blocks) and α-l-glucuronic acid (G-blocks) in different percentages [13]. Alginates are non-toxic, cost-effective, highly water-absorbent, biocompatible, inherently non-stick, available, and biodegradable [12,14]. Binding between cationic chitosan and anionic alginate is an effective method for the preparation of composites from these two biopolymers. Alginate/chitosan composite materials were found to improve wound healing by boosting the formation of red blood cells and platelets and then producing blood clots [15]. In addition, the alginate/chitosan combination aids in ECM secretion and granulation tissue development. Other properties of this composite that are important in wound healing include antibacterial properties and the ability to absorb a substantial volume of wound exudate [16]. However, this composite material still has significant drawbacks.

Nanomaterials have a lot of potential for multipurpose applications because of their microscopic size and extraordinarily large surface area [17]. Carbon dots (CDs) with a size of fewer than 10 nm have found many applications in biomedical and healthcare applications due to their hydrophilic nature, good water solubility, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, strong mechanical strength, antibacterial effects, and Ease of production [18]. Best of our knowledge, there is no report on the fabrication of nanocomposite sponges with chitosan, sodium alginate, and CDs. In this study, the Pyracantha plant as a natural precursor was used for the synthesis of CDs by the hydrothermal method, and its general characteristics are investigated. Subsequently, CDs were used to be trapped in the biopolymer sponges of chitosan and sodium alginate. Electrostatic interaction, crosslinking with calcium chloride, and freeze-drying procedures were used to create alginate/chitosan/CDs (ACCD) nanocomposite sponges. The characteristics of nanocomposite sponges were determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Water vapor transmission rate, water absorption, water-holding capacity, porosity, mechanical properties, and contact angle analysis. Blood compatibility and cytotoxicity of nanocomposite sponge were also investigated. In addition, a blood clotting index was used to assess in vitro blood compatibility.

The fruit of the Pyracantha was used to synthesize CDs by the one-step hydrothermal method [19]. Briefly, 2 g of the precursor with 20 mL of DI water was placed in a stainless steel autoclave and then was placed at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, a dark brown solution was obtained, which was filtered through filter paper. The resulting solution was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove larger particles. Finally, the supernatant solution was collected and passed through a 0.22 μm filter and was kept at 4 °C for further characterization and evaluation (Fig. 1 ).

LAMBDA 950 UV–VIS spectrophotometer was used to acquire UV–VIS absorption spectra (PerkinElmer, America). Fluorescence measurements were performed at room temperature with a PerkinElmer LS-45 fluorescence spectrometer. Tensor 27 (Bruker, Germany) FT-IR spectra were collected in the 400–4000 cm−1 range using the KBr pellet method. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a CM300 electron microscope at a 200 kV acceleration voltage was used to examine the morphologies and size of the CDs. A 30 min ultrasonic exposure was followed by cautious placement of the CD solutions over a copper mesh coated with a microscope's coating. The CD size distribution was determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) which was studied using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS in automated mode with a scattering angle of 90° and a He–Ne laser light source with 10 mW maximum intensity and a wavelength of 632.8 nm. To determine crystallinity, X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) was performed on the synthesized CDs. With the use of the PANalytical (XPert PRo MPD, Netherlands) equipment, the XRD pattern could be observed. X-ray pattern with Cu K radiation (λ = 1.54060) and a 40 kV and 40 mA voltage target. Teksan (TakRam N1-541, Iran) spectrometer was used to record the Raman spectra. The sample was stimulated with the laser sources of 520 nm with a resolution of <1.5 cm−1. The synthesized CDs solution was coated over a newly prepared glass plate and dried for 2 h at room temperature. A baseline correction was performed on the spectrum to enhance the peak intensity.

With a few adjustments, the previously published procedures were used to prepare the ACCD sponges [15]. Alginate (2% w/v) was added to 100 mL of deionized (DI) water and stirred using a mechanical over stirrer for 2 h to create a homogenous solution for the preparation of composite sponges. This is followed by dissolving the chitosan (1% w/v) for 2 h in 50 mL of 1% acetic acid solution with the aid of a mechanical stirrer and adding alginate slowly to the chitosan solution. At room temperature, the homogenous gel solution was stirred at 500 rpm for 2 h. Simultaneously, glycerol (2% v/v) was gradually added to the mixture. Furthermore, the CDs were mixed mechanically for 1 h to create homogenous solutions at concentrations of 0, 0.5, 1, 2, and 2%. After freezing at −20 °C for 24 h, 10 mL of these gel solutions were lyophilized under vacuum for 48 h at −60 °C in a freeze-dryer (Christ Alpha1-2®, Germany) at 0.12 mbar. These structures were immersed in a CaCl2/NaOH/Ethanol solution (m (CaCl2 0.1 M): m (NaOH 0.1 M): m (97% Ethanol) = 10:20:70 solution for 6 h for cross-linking and pH neutralization, then washed with an excess of DI water and lyophilized for another 24 h to obtain ACCD0, ACCD1, ACCD2, and ACCD3 nanocomposite sponges, respectively (Fig. 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the ACCD nanocomposite sponges

ce:section.ce:para: A scanning electron microscope (SEM; Tescan Mira3, Czech Republic) with a 10 kV acceleration voltage was used to examine the surface morphology and porous structure. To enhance conductivity, samples were sputter-coated with platinum before imaging.

The infrared spectrum of the sponges was measured using FT-IR spectroscopy. Tensor 27 (Bruker, Germany) FT-IR spectra were collected in the 400–4000 cm−1 range at room temperature with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

Contact angle (Ɵ) was measured to determine the hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity of nanocomposite sponges [20]. For this purpose, a very small drop (4 μL) of DI water was placed on 3 different positions of the sponge surface and a contact angle analyzer (Data-physics Instruments, GmbH, Germany) was measured the contact angle.

The porosity of nanocomposite sponges was determined using the liquid displacement technique [21]. Briefly, sponge samples were saturated in 100% ethanol. Before and after immersion in ethanol, the weight of the sponge samples was determined. The formula (1) is used to calculate porosity:
(1)
P ( % ) =
W 2
-
W 1
ρ V
× 100 %
The weights of the samples before and after immersion in ethanol are W1 and W2, respectively. V denotes the sample volume before immersion, and ρ is the ethanol density.

To prevent water vapor loss through the boundary, the nanocomposite sponges were firmly wrapped around the mouth of a cylindrical bottle containing 10 mL of DI water and secured with Teflon tape across the edge. They were weighed and stored at 37 °C and 50% humidity for 24 h. Finally, they were re-weighed. The weight loss of the whole system was considered as an index of WVTR by the following formula (2):
(2)
W V T R
( % )
=
W i
−
W f
A
× 100 %
( g /
m 2
/ 24 h )
A represents the size of the vial opening in square centimeters (cm2), Wi is its original weight in grams, and Wf is its ending weight in grams with sponges on top.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water absorption and water retention capacity
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate the sponge water absorption capacity, a piece of sponge was immersed in water at room temperature until it was saturated with liquid [8]. The sponge was soaked in phosphate buffer saline (pH 7.4) until no weight change was detected. Formula (3) was used to compute the liquid adsorption capacity (L):
(3)
L
( % )
=
m 1
−
m 0
m 0
× 100 %
The dressing quality before and after PBS absorption is represented by m0 (g) and m1 (g).

According to a recent report [21], water retention capacity was also tested. The sample was put in a desiccator at 37 °C for 24 h and weighed as m2. To calculate the sponge's water-retaining capacity (R), we used the formula (4)
(4)
R
( % )
=
m 2
−
m 0
m 1
−
m 0
× 100 %

Using the ASTM D882-91 standard technique, the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (E) of nanocomposite sponges were determined. The maximal force (N) per unit area (mm2) necessary to shatter a sample was measured in MPa. E is the percentage ratio of the length of the sample film at maximum stress to the original size. The test was carried out on a worldwide test machine with a 100 kg load cell moving at a speed of 10 mm/min. Nanocomposite sponges with size of 20 × 40 × 1.5 mm were manufactured, and clamps were used to secure the ends of the films and sponges. The results were based on the average of three measurements of samples. The following formulas were used to obtain tensile strength and elongation at break of sponges [22]:
(5)
Tensile
strength =
F
m a x
A
(6)
Elongation
at
break
( % )
=
L -
L 0
L 0
× 100 %

A shows the cross-sectional area, L0 represents the initial length of the samples before the test, L indicates the final length at break, Fmax shows the maximum force applied by the sample in the tensile test.

The sponges were sterilized for 30 min under UV light. DMEM with 10% FBS, penicillin (100 U/mL), and streptomycin (100 g/mL) were added to a 96-well plate with 5 × 105 cells per well, and the cells were cultured in 5% CO2 at 37 °C overnight. Furthermore, the medium was discarded. Fresh medium was added after nanocomposite sponges of the same size (5 mm) were put slightly on top of cells. As a control, wells containing only cells were utilized. After 1, 2, and 3 days, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL) was applied directly to each well medium, resulting in a final concentration of 2 mg/mL. The media containing MTT was removed after 4 h of incubation at 37 °C, and 100 μL DMSO was added. Finally, using a microplate reader (Tecan Austria GmbH Untersbergstrasse 1A A-5082, Austria), the absorbance of the solution in the well at 570 nm was measured at 492 nm (655 nm as reference). The formula (7) was used to calculate cell viability:
(7)
Cell
viability
( % )
=
S a m p l e
a b
s
492 - 655
C o n t r o l
a b
s
492 - 655
× 100 %

The hemolysis ratio of the samples was examined in vitro by contact method to determine sponge compatibility with blood [23]. First, 5 mL of fresh rat blood (4–6 weeks old) was gently put into a tube containing 0.5 mL of sodium citrate (3.8%) anticoagulant solution. Anticoagulant blood (0.2 mL) was added to a physiological saline solution comprising various samples in a 10 mL volume. 0.2 mL of fresh blood was added to DI water and physiological saline, respectively, to make positive and negative control samples. At 37 °C, all of the samples were incubated. After 2 h, the solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 1500 rpm, and the absorbance of the supernatant on each tube was measured using a microplate reader at 540 nm (BioTek Instruments, Epoch2, Winooski, VT, USA). The following equation was used to compute the hemolysis rate:
(8)
B l o o d
h e m o l y s i s
( % )
=
O D s − O D n
O D p − O D n
× 100 %
The optical density of a tested sample is ODS, the negative control's optical density is ODn, and the positive control optical density is ODP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Whole blood clotting tests in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
As previously reported [1], blood clotting studies were performed in vitro. A total of 100 μl of citrated whole blood was dispersed over 100 mg of each sponge, followed by 20 mL of 0.2 M CaCl2 solution. After that, the sponge was incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. The dish was then washed with 25 mL of DI water to remove the uncoagulated blood. Finally, uncoagulated blood solution absorption was measured at 540 nm (abs1). As a control, 100 μl of whole citrated blood was measured in 25 mL of DI water (abs0). The following Eq. (9) was used to determine the blood clotting index (BCI):
(9)
BCI
( % )
=
a b
s 1
a b
s 0
× 100
All results were reported as mean standard deviation. At P 0.05, the significant difference was detected using the Student t-test.

CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using Pyracantha fruits as a green precursor and DI water as a solvent. The diluted solution of CDs is pale yellow under sunlight, while under the 365 nm UV lamp turns blue. A large peak can be seen at 218 nm which is due to π –π∗ transitions of the conjugated electron, while another peak at about 273 nm is due to n–π∗ transitions from the oxygen atom or heteroatom (Fig. 3 a) [24]. PL spectrum showed a strong peak of about 440 nm when the excitation wavelength is 370 nm. We have also prepared CDs at different times (2–7 h). The highest emission peak was observed at 200 °C and 6 h because a large number of particles are carbonized at this temperature (Fig. 3b). FT-IR spectroscopy was performed to study the functional groups present on the surface of CDs. In the FT-IR analysis of CDs, the following are observed: O–H at 3300 cm−1, C–H at 2925 cm−1, CO at 1650 cm−1, N–H at 1580 cm−1, CC at 1400 cm−1, C–O at 1045 cm−1, =C–H at 700 cm−1 (Fig. 3c) [25].

The size and morphology of CDs were determined using TEM and DLS. The particle size distribution was obtained at about 4 nm (Fig. 4 a). TEM image (Fig. 4b) shows that the CDs are spherical in structure and have a uniform dispersion. Fig. 4c shows the results of XRD measurements. The CDs displayed a diffraction major peak at 20.12°. This is due to the presence of graphite carbon, which indicates that the synthesized green CDs have a weak crystal structure and are amorphous in nature [26]. The CDs Raman spectra have two peaks at 1388 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1, which corresponds to the D and G bands, respectively (Fig. 4d). The vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds on the termination plane of disordered graphite are related to the D band. The vibration of sp2 carbon atoms in a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice is related to the G band. The D to G band ratio (ID/IG) shows the degree of a flaw in the graphitic structure of CDs. Since corrosion and oxygenation processes are reduced in CDs with lower ID values, the graphite shells will be more integrated resulting in higher levels of graphitization [24]. CDs have an ID/IG ratio of 0.87, indicating that sp2 band formation is at an acceptable level when compared to structural flaws [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of ACCD nanocomposite sponges
ce:section.ce:para: ACCD nanocomposite sponges with four different amounts of CDs (0, 0.5,1, and 2%) were successfully prepared via Ca2+ crosslinking, electrostatic interaction, and freeze-drying procedures. The negatively charged alginate can interact with the cationic charged amine group of the chitosan unit to form a polyelectrolyte mixture [21]. More complicated ionic interactions may be achieved with the inclusion of negative and positive charges of functional groups at the surface of CDs into the alginate/chitosan (AC) solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The surface morphology and porous structure
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 a, AC sponges are white, but ACCD sponges are pale brown with a homogeneous distribution, indicating a uniform distribution of CDs. It was also shown that nanocomposite sponges have a highly porous structure whose cavities were identical in shape and size and had irregular grids. On the other hand, most of the cavities had an interconnected structure. Also, by comparing the microscopic images of ACCD0 and ACCD3 sponges, it was concluded that adding CDs increases the size of the pores and reduces their number, which can be clearly seen in Fig. 5b and c. CDs have increased crosslinking in sponges due to the positive and negative charge functional groups. In general, the distributed micropores and porous channel structures in sponges can directly affect porosity, water absorption and retention, gas permeability, and mechanical strength, and should have positive effects on platelet adhesion, accumulation of red blood cells, and sufficient space for gas transfer. Therefore, they increase the absorption and penetration of blood cells and faster blood coagulation after injury [28].

FT-IR spectroscopy is a common method for identifying the functional groups of ACCD sponges confirming the presence of CDs and identifying the link between that and the functional groups at the polymer surface. The peaks observed at 3281 cm−1 and 3282 cm−1 (Fig. 6 a) indicate that both composites have OH groups that may be derived from alginate and chitosan [29]. On the other hand, according to the diagram in Fig. 3c, this peak could be related to OH at the surface of CDs. This wide peak indicates that intermolecular hydrogen bonds are formed in the ACCD nanocomposites. The other peak is around 2880 cm−1, which is related to the stretching CH group, the intensity of it has increased after the addition of CDs, which indicates the presence of CDs in the composite. The two peaks are around 1400 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1, which can be related to groups C–C and N–H, increased in intensity with the addition of CDs. On the other hand, these absorption peaks can be related to asymmetric and symmetrical tensile vibrations of carboxylic groups from alginate. Also, a peak of about 1550 cm−1 could be related to the bending vibration –NH (amide) of chitosan [21]. The electrostatic interaction between the alginate carboxylate group and the chitosan amine group results in the formation of a polyelectrolyte complex [29]. The strongest peak observed at 1027 cm−1 belongs to the secondary hydroxyl group (characteristic −CH–OH peak in cyclic alcohols, CO traction) and the primary hydroxyl group (characteristic –CH2–OH peak in primary alcohols, CO traction) [30].

Proper hydrophilicity is one of the most important and necessary key factors for an ideal wound dressing. Wound dressing with a hydrophilic surface can absorb excess wound exudates and maintain a moist environment to facilitate wound closure and the healing process [31]. Fig. 6b shows the contact angles of the ACCD sponges. According to the diagram, the contact angle decreases with the increasing concentration of CDs. So that the contact angle changed from 113° for the ACCD0 sample to 85° for the ACCD3 sample. Hydrophilic CDs, due to their hydrophilic functional groups, have altered the wettability capability of nanocomposite sponges and increased their hydrophilic properties [24]. Therefore, by increasing the concentration of CDs, not only does the hydrophilicity of ACCD increase significantly, but it can also affect water absorption and retention.

The mechanical characteristics and rate of absorption of blood, proteins, and lipids are both affected by porosity, which is an important component of wound dressings. Improved porosity can help keep wounds moist while also increasing cell proliferation and wound healing [14]. The porosity of the ACCD sponge increased with increasing CDs concentration from 65% to 103%, which showed a statistically significant difference (Fig. 7 a). This increase in porosity was consistent with the results of SEM images. The findings revealed that the samples had a highly linked and porous morphology with an uneven layout, allowing enough space for fluids and blood to absorb and so speed wound healing. Porosity is also beneficial for delivering nutrients and oxygen to wound dressing cells [8].

Water absorption and retention are essential to wound dressing qualities that can be utilized to assess the effectiveness of wound dressings in removing wound exudation and maintaining a moist environment around the wound. Moisture retention in the wound bed is often regarded as the most favorable condition for wound healing [8]. Also, an excellent hemostatic substance should have strong blood absorption properties, which can rapidly absorb the outflow of blood to facilitate platelet aggregation and stimulate coagulation factors to achieve rapid homeostasis [32]. The water absorption of all composite sponges is shown in Fig. 7b. With the increasing concentration of CDs, water absorption increases, which can also be due to the hydrophilicity of CDs. To evaluate the water holding capacity of nanocomposite sponges, the weight changes of these swollen sponges were measured at 37 °C and 60% humidity. ACCD3 and ACCD2 sponges have better holding capacity compared to ACCD and ACCD1. Therefore, adding a certain amount of CDs to the ACCD0 sponge can improve water retention (Fig. 7c).

The WVTR test is a good approach to see if a wound dressing can limit water loss and gas passage through sponges or other dressings, which is an important physical attribute of wound dressings that can control the microenvironment during wound healing. The presence of pores allows for high gas penetration and good wound protection against external contamination and dehydration, and it is critical for the lesion to breathe and control the microenvironment in order to cure the wound [33]. According to previous reports [34], dressings with WVTR of about 2000–2500 g/m2/24 h can retain moisture well and thus provide a favorable environment for the growth of epidermal cells and fibroblasts. Lower WVTR may increase infection and poor wound healing due to poor drainage of absorbed exudates, whereas excessive WVTR may lead to dryness of the wound surface due to excessive fluid loss as water vapor [35]. As shown in Fig. 7d, the WVTR rates for samples ACCD0, ACCD1, ACCD2, and ACCD3 were (1891 ± 251 g/m2/24 h), (2011 ± 213 g/m2/24 h), (2354 ± 321 g/m2/24 h) and (2611 ± 267 g/m2/24 h), respectively. In general, as the concentration of CDs increases, the WVTR increases, which may be due to the increase in pore diameter and porosity rate in nanocomposites combined with CDs. On the other hand, this increase can be related to the hydrophilicity of CDs [24]. Therefore, all ACCD nanocomposite sponges were suitable for wound dressing and maintained a moist environment that could accelerate wound healing.

In order to be used as a wound dressing, mechanical properties are essential to provide adequate physical support for cell growth and their application to homeostasis. Therefore, it is very important for an ideal wound dressing to have proper mechanical properties [31]. Nanocomposite sponges with good mechanical qualities can not only tolerate external force but also keep their integrity, preventing skin damage [14]. The mechanical properties of ACCD sponges such as tensile strength, elongation at break, and extension based on the force were investigated. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the tensile strength of ACCD sponges showed a significant increase after the addition of CDs. A tensile strength of ACCD0, ACCD1, ACCD2, and ACCD3 were 0.2249, 0.4935, 0.4379, and 0.3986, respectively. However, with increasing CDs, there was no continuous increase in tensile strength, and ACCD1 showed the highest tensile strength. Also, according to Fig. 8b, the elongation at break of ACCCD0, ACCCD1, ACCCD2, and ACCCD3 sponges were 20, 39, 48, and 36%, respectively showing an increase with the addition of CDs. On the other hand, the modulus of these sponges is shown in Fig. 8c. Unlike previous experiments, the addition of CDs reduced the modulus of the sponges. Finally, the bending strain ability of ACCD sponges is shown in Fig. 8d. According to the diagram, the ACCD1 sponge showed the highest bending strain ability. In general, the addition of CDs increased the bending strain ability of nanocomposites [14]. The increase in mechanical strength of nanocomposite sponges compared to composite sponges without CDs can be due to the strong electrostatic interaction between the polymer matrix of the polymers and the CDs [36]. In summary, the use of CDs in polymer matrices and the formation of nanocomposite sponges may provide the appropriate mechanical properties required for an ideal wound dressing and make them more suitable for wound healing.

The investigation of cytotoxicity is critical for any dressing used for wound healing [37]. This chemical must promote cell adhesion and proliferation while also being non-toxic, non-allergic, and biocompatible to obtain the optimum wound dressing [38]. On the other hand, the non-toxic behavior of CDs can indicate the potential application of CDs in nanocomposites. MTT assay was performed to evaluate the effect of ACCD sponges on L929 fibroblast cell growth. The MTT result in terms of cell viability is shown in Fig. 9 a. Positive control had the highest absorption after 24, 48, and 72 h. At the beginning and after 24 h of incubation, the cell viability for ACCD1 and ACCD2 sponges increased compared to ACCD0, but for ACCD3 sponges, a significant decrease of about 82% was recorded. However, after 48 h, cell viability for ACCD3 nanocomposite sponges increased significantly by about 99%. َACCD0 sponge also showed the lowest cell viability compared to others. During the 72 h, ACCD3 showed a higher cell viability rate than the others. The results showed that fibroblast L929 cell growth was not inhibited by ACCD sponges and in all incubation periods and for all sponges, the cell viability was recorded between 82% and 99%. A cytotoxic chemical is defined as one in which the survival of cells exposed to it for 24 h is less than 70% according to ISO-10993-5-2009 criteria.

Hemolysis is a simple and reliable method for assessing the blood compatibility of wound dressings [1]. Hemolysis is described as the release of hemoglobin into the plasma as a result of red blood cell injury, and it is directly related to the material's blood compatibility [40]. After incubation with rat blood for 1 h at 37 °C, the hemolysis rate of ACCD nanocomposite wound dressings was determined during contact with blood. According to ASTM F756-00, substances having a hemolytic index of 0–2% are categorized as non-hemolytic, those with 2–5% are classified as minor hemolytic, and those with more than 5% are classified as hemolytic [41]. Sponge composites without CDs had a hemolysis rate of 1.94%, which rose with the addition of CDs to 3.68% in ACCD3 nanocomposite sponges (Fig. 9b). The results revealed that adding up to 2% of CDs to the sponges did not change their non-hemolytic capabilities. According to the above definition, all of the materials given had a hemolytic rate of less than 5%, indicating compatibility with sufficient blood and acceptability for medical usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Whole blood clotting tests in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Because extensive thick wounds can result in heavy bleeding that is difficult to control, the wound dressing excellent hemostatic function is particularly important for expediting wound healing [21]. To quantitatively explore their coagulation properties and evaluate their hemostatic function, the relative BCI of ACCD sponge wound dressings was determined utilizing in vitro blood clotting methods. The smaller the BCI, the greater the material's hemostatic potential [1]. The water used to wash sponges with blood is not red, as shown in Fig. 9c, suggesting that blood can coagulate well on all sponges. Hemoglobin concentration in all samples was assessed by quantitative measurement of OD values of hemoglobin solutions utilizing BCI to better understand the hemostatic performance of nanocomposite sponges. The BCI index decreases when the concentration of CDs in nanocomposite sponges increases. As a result, the indexes for sponges ACCD0, ACCD1, ACCD2, and ACCD3 were 26, 22.3, 18.6, and 13.3, respectively (Fig. 9c). This is because nanocomposite sponges have higher porosity, water absorption and retention, and mechanical strength than composite sponges. All of which are desired for maintaining a three-dimensional porous structure [1]. Simultaneously, we believe that the cationic component of chitosan and the CDs in nanocomposite sponges can better generate a viscous blood-shaped gel, which will improve blood coagulation.

In conclusion, we have successfully reported the method of making ACCD nanocomposite sponges by Ca2+ crosslinking, electrostatic interaction, and lyophilization with interesting properties. The CDs used to make the nanocomposite are made from Pyracantha by the easy and one-step hydrothermal method. The main criterion for the production of these nanocomposites using freeze-drying methods was the successful electrostatic interaction between the positive charge of chitosan, the negative charge of alginate, and the negative charge of CDs. These electrostatic interactions helped the ACCD nanocomposites to have superior properties compared to the AC composites such as mechanical properties, water absorption, water vapor transmission rate, and higher hydrophilicity. On the other hand, the results showed that ACCD nanocomposites are non-toxic to fibroblast cells and have good biocompatibility. These nanocomposite sponges also exhibit good blood clotting ability, low hemolysis rate, and high water absorption and retention capacity, making them a good choice for wound dressing applications. Therefore, by adjusting the concentration of CDs in nanocomposite sponges, ways to create sponges with interesting and desirable properties are provided. The results make ACCD nanocomposite sponges good candidates for use as potent wound dressings.

Ali Rezaei: Investigation, Resources, Visualization, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing. Hamide Ehtesabi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to those who supported them especially vice presidency for research and technology, Shahid Beheshti University.
